codeSnapshot.label: outlier_detection.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.ensemble import IsolationForest
from sklearn.covariance import EllipticEnvelope

X = df[['age', 'income', 'spend']].values

# ── Univariate: IQR method ──
Q1 = df['income'].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = df['income'].quantile(0.75)
IQR = Q3 - Q1
lower, upper = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR, Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
outliers_iqr = df[(df['income'] < lower) | (df['income'] > upper)]
print(f'IQR outliers: {len(outliers_iqr)}')

# ── Univariate: Z-score (assumes normality) ──
z_scores = np.abs(stats.zscore(df['income'].dropna()))
outliers_z = df[z_scores > 3]  # |z| > 3 ≈ 0.27% false-positive rate for normal data

# ── Multivariate: Mahalanobis distance ──
cov = np.cov(X.T)
VI = np.linalg.pinv(cov)  # inverse covariance
diff = X - X.mean(axis=0)
mahal = np.sqrt(np.einsum('ij,jk,ik->i', diff, VI, diff))
# Under multivariate normal, D² ~ Chi²(d); threshold: chi2.ppf(0.975, df=3)
from scipy.stats import chi2
threshold = np.sqrt(chi2.ppf(0.975, df=X.shape[1]))
outliers_mahal = np.where(mahal > threshold)[0]

# ── Multivariate: Isolation Forest ──
isoforest = IsolationForest(contamination=0.05, random_state=42)
labels = isoforest.fit_predict(X)  # -1 = outlier, 1 = inlier
outliers_if = df[labels == -1]
print(f'Isolation Forest outliers: {len(outliers_if)}')

# ── Robust covariance (EllipticEnvelope) ──
env = EllipticEnvelope(contamination=0.05, random_state=42)
env.fit(X)
labels_env = env.predict(X)

eli3: An outlier is a data point that is far from the rest — an unusually high income, a transaction amount that is 100× the typical value, a sensor reading from a broken instrument. Outliers can be real signal (a genuine unusual case) or data errors (typos, sensor malfunctions, ETL bugs).

Why does it matter? Outliers inflate the mean and standard deviation, distort linear model coefficients (a single extreme point can move a regression line substantially), and confuse distance-based algorithms like k-NN and k-means. Whether to remove, transform, or preserve outliers depends on understanding why they exist.

Detection methods range from simple rules (this value is more than 3 standard deviations from the mean) to sophisticated algorithms (Isolation Forest, which detects outliers by measuring how easily a point is isolated from the rest). The right method depends on dimensionality and whether outliers are isolated points or small clusters.

whenWhy.title: Data cleaning before model training
whenWhy.body: Outlier detection is a standard preprocessing step before fitting linear models, k-NN, and k-means. These methods are sensitive to extreme values: one outlier can shift a linear regression coefficient substantially. For tree-based models (Random Forest, XGBoost), outliers have less impact since splits are based on rank, not magnitude. Always examine flagged outliers manually before deleting — many 'outliers' are legitimate extreme cases.

whenWhy.title: Anomaly detection as a ML task
whenWhy.body: Fraud detection, network intrusion detection, and manufacturing defect detection treat outlier identification as the primary task, not preprocessing. Here the 'outlier' is the signal. Algorithms: Isolation Forest (fast, works in high dimensions), Local Outlier Factor (density-based, detects contextual anomalies), Autoencoders (reconstruct normal patterns; high reconstruction error = anomaly). See the anomaly detection ML category for the full treatment.

whenWhy.title: Robust statistics and feature engineering
whenWhy.body: Rather than removing outliers, you can use robust statistics: median instead of mean, IQR instead of standard deviation, robust scalers (RobustScaler in sklearn uses Q1/Q3). Log-transforming right-skewed distributions (income, transaction amounts) reduces the influence of extreme values without deletion. Winsorizing (capping values at the 1st/99th percentile) preserves all rows while limiting outlier leverage.

deepDiveIntro: Chandola, Banerjee & Kumar (2009) distinguish three outlier types: (1) Point outliers — an individual observation deviates significantly from the rest (a single transaction of $1,000,000 in a dataset where the 99th percentile is $500). (2) Contextual (conditional) outliers — a value is anomalous in context but not globally (30°C is normal in July but anomalous in January for the same location). (3) Collective outliers — a group of observations is anomalous together (a sequence of HTTP requests forming a port scan, where each individual request is normal). [1]

Detection methods divide by dimensionality. Univariate: IQR rule (box-plot whiskers at Q1 − 1.5·IQR, Q3 + 1.5·IQR) and Z-score (|z| > 3). Both assume roughly symmetric distributions; for skewed data, apply log-transform first or use the modified Z-score with median absolute deviation (MAD). Multivariate: Mahalanobis distance accounts for correlations between features; Isolation Forest is distribution-free and scales to high dimensions. [2]

deepDive.heading: IQR and Z-Score Methods
deepDive.body: IQR method: define outlier as any point outside [Q1 − 1.5·IQR, Q3 + 1.5·IQR]. The 1.5 multiplier comes from Tukey (1977): for a normal distribution it flags ~0.7% of points as outliers. The 3.0 multiplier (extreme outliers) flags ~0.01%. The IQR method is non-parametric — no distributional assumption. Z-score: z = (x − μ) / σ; |z| > 3 is the standard threshold (flags 0.27% under normality). Both methods are strictly univariate — they examine one variable at a time and miss multivariate outliers (a point that is normal on each axis individually but anomalous in the joint space). [2]
deepDive.formula: IQR = Q3 − Q1;  lower = Q1 − 1.5·IQR;  upper = Q3 + 1.5·IQR

deepDive.heading: Mahalanobis Distance
deepDive.body: For a point x with population mean μ and covariance Σ, the Mahalanobis distance is D_M(x) = √((x−μ)ᵀ Σ⁻¹ (x−μ)). It measures how many standard deviations x is from μ in the direction of x, accounting for correlations. Under multivariate normality, D_M² follows a chi-squared distribution with d degrees of freedom. Threshold: D_M > √χ²_{0.975, d} (the 97.5th percentile). Limitation: Σ⁻¹ is distorted by outliers themselves (masking effect). Solution: use the Minimum Covariance Determinant (MCD) robust estimator via sklearn's EllipticEnvelope. [2]
deepDive.formula: D_M(x) = √((x−μ)ᵀ Σ⁻¹ (x−μ));   D_M² ~ χ²(d) under multivariate normality

deepDive.heading: Isolation Forest
deepDive.body: Algorithm: build an ensemble of random decision trees, each constructed by recursively selecting a random feature and a random split point. Outliers — being rare and different — require fewer splits to isolate than normal points. The anomaly score is the average path length to isolation across trees, normalized to [0, 1]. Threshold via contamination parameter: fraction of expected outliers. Properties: O(n·t·ψ) time where t = number of trees, ψ = subsample size (default 256). Works well in high dimensions; does not require an assumed distribution. Original paper: Liu, Ting & Zhou, ICDM 2008. [1]

takeaways: Three outlier types: point, contextual, collective. Choose detection method based on type and dimensionality. [1]
IQR (non-parametric, univariate): lower = Q1 − 1.5·IQR, upper = Q3 + 1.5·IQR. Z-score (parametric, univariate): |z| > 3. [2]
Mahalanobis distance (multivariate, parametric): D_M² ~ χ²(d). Use EllipticEnvelope for outlier-robust covariance estimation. [2]
Isolation Forest: distribution-free, scales to high dimensions, O(n·t·ψ). Score ≈ average path length to isolation. [1]
Before deleting outliers: verify they're errors, not genuine extreme cases. For skewed data, log-transform or winsorize rather than delete. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the masking effect in outlier detection, and how do you address it?
edgeCases.a: Masking occurs when multiple outliers influence the sample mean and covariance, making each other appear less extreme than they truly are. Example: if 5% of data are outliers clustered together, the sample mean shifts toward them, reducing their z-scores. Classical Z-score and Mahalanobis with sample covariance both suffer from masking. Solutions: (1) Minimum Covariance Determinant (MCD) estimator — finds the h-subset (h ≈ 0.75n) that minimizes the determinant of the covariance matrix, giving a robust estimate. sklearn's EllipticEnvelope uses MCD. (2) Sequential outlier removal: flag and remove one outlier at a time, refit statistics, repeat. Computationally expensive but effective.

edgeCases.q: Should you remove outliers for tree-based models?
edgeCases.a: Generally, no. Decision trees split on thresholds and are not affected by the magnitude of values — only whether a point falls above or below the split threshold. XGBoost and Random Forest have similar robustness. An outlier in a tree model simply gets placed in the correct leaf without influencing other predictions. Removing outliers before tree-based models can actually hurt performance if those outliers are genuine observations the model should learn to predict. Exception: target variable (y) outliers — a single extremely high y value dominates squared-error loss in regression. Log-transforming the target (predict log(y), exponentiate) or using Huber loss (robust to outliers) is more principled than deletion.

sources.label: Chandola, V., Banerjee, A. & Kumar, V. — Anomaly Detection: A Survey, ACM CSUR 41(3), 2009
